text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'll talk about how to build rag systems for the real world. I'll start with a recap of Rag and where people go wrong with it and then walk through a five-step approach for building rag apps systematically. If you're watching this video, you've probably heard of Rag before, but just to give a quick recap, Rag is short for retrieval augmented generation. And all this is is an AI system whose responses are grounded in the right context automatically. Just to show this visually, we have our large language model here. And instead of just having it rely on its out ofthe-box knowledge, we give it access to a set of source documents or some kind of knowledge base that can be automatically added to the context window. So these can be things like text files and word documents and PDFs. But they can also be all different kinds of context. They can be books, they can be employee manuals, it can be documentation, it can be video transcripts, it can be meeting notes, it can even be images and non-ext data. So in RG, we automatically grab the right context, give it to the LLM so it can generate better responses. Some of the big reasons why RAG is helpful is first and foremost, it helps mitigate hallucinations. Hallucinations are where LLMs make things up, not because they're trying to lie or anything, but just the way they're developed. They're text generators and they are aligned to be more helpful. They try to be helpful and they try to make you happy, but sometimes that means coming up with an answer even if the answer isn't fully grounded in reality. So by automatically providing the model with the right context, we can reduce the probability that the LLM makes something up when we don't want it to. Another benefit is that rag is a relatively easy and reliable way to manage the knowledge of your AI system. So instead of trying to do things like fine-tuning to teach the model specific things, you can just give it a knowledge base with the specific knowledge you want it to have. And then finally, even though LLMs know a lot about the world, they don't know everything. They don't have knowledge about things that happened after their training cut off which might be 6 12 or even 18 months in the past but also they don't have access to proprietary information. So if you are trying to deploy this rag system in the context of a business and the business has a lot of internal information that's not publicly available then the LLM will never have seen that because it would not have made it into its pre-training data. While at this point it might sound like Rag solves so many of the issues that people face when trying to use AI systems to solve business problems and the like, there's a common problem that comes up when people try to deploy Rag systems for realworld use cases, which ultimately boils down to this. They're able to spin up impressive Rag demos, but their deployments are disappointing. So the story typically goes like this. someone hears about rag and how it can solve so many of their problems and they can create some kind of internal knowledge base for their company. So what they do is they go grab a bunch of documents, they dump it in a vector database using any one of the many vector database tools available these days and they build this retrieval system so the LLM can grab context and answer questions. And so this works really well in the demo where they're asking like demo questions like what is X? How do I do Y? when was the last time Z happened? So they basically ask questions that they know the LLM will be able to answer. But when you deploy this in the real world context, the system doesn't work as well. So there are many reasons why RAG systems fail. But the question we want to answer here is how do we build these systems for the real world in a reliable way? That brings up this five-step RAG framework that I'm going to walk through. So the first step is to scope the MVP. Step two is to create a so-called golden data set. Step three is to build the initial version of your retrieval system. Step four is to build the initial version of your answer system. And then the final step is to run experiments. And so the first step is to scope the MVP. This first step really has nothing to do with AI or machine learning or technology or coding or software, anything like that. This is just project management 101. But interestingly, these days there seems to be so much excitement and pressure and innovation in the AI space that even experienced managers forget about the fundamental basics of project management. In other words, they just jump to building the rag system without properly doing project discovery. And so when it comes to scoping the rag system, there are three key things I want to think about. The first is who are the users? who's going to actually be using the system. Are they technical people? Are they non-technical people? What roles do they have? What teams do they sit on? All these kinds of things. Because this context is important and is going to have design implications for your system. The second is the use case. What problem are these users trying to solve? Is this just like customer support? Is this for some kind of shopping experience? Is this like a legal use case? Is it an accounting use case? Is it insurance claims? What's the scope of the system? What are the things that this system needs to be able to do? And perhaps more importantly, what are the things that this system is not going to do? And then finally, once you have an understanding of the user and the use case, then selecting the inventory. So basically picking the source data that's going to live in the knowledge base of the retrieval system. One common pitfall that happens here is that people will overcope this initial version. They try to create a rag system that serves all users, all use cases and has all possible data sources. And the reality is building such a system is difficult and timeconuming and a massive investment and may not generate an impact that justifies that investment. However, lucky for us, in almost every situation, you don't need to build a system that serves all users, all use cases, and includes all possible source documents. Because out of all the possible rag systems you can implement, there's a very small percentage that generates most of the impact or most of the value. And so if you're building a rag system for the first time at your company, for example, it's important that you focus on those high lever use cases for which you're serving only a single user, a single use case, and maybe one or two data sources, but generates most of the impact of that imaginary super rag system. To give you a concrete example, I've been working on this answer engine using my YouTube videos. And so for this system, the users are students of my AI cohort. The use case is specifically technical AI Q&A. So not answering career questions or entrepreneurship questions or client management questions, strictly technical Q&A. And then finally, the inventory here are just my YouTube video transcripts where I have a lot of technical content. So this one user, this one use case and this one data source is a great starting place because this is going to be the seed for expanding this system whether it's expanding the user base or expanding the use cases or expanding the data sources. And so a pro tip here is to start simple and specific because if you overcope the MVP, one of two things is going to happen. one, you're not going to build a great system because you just don't have enough time to give each component of the system the attention that it needs. Or two, you don't have enough time to actually build the thing properly. So, the project stalls halfway through and then it just gets scrapped. But if you start simple and specific, you actually give yourself a chance to implement the system in a reasonable time frame, like let's say 6 weeks. And at the end of that six weeks, you'll have a pretty good idea of what the next steps should be for that project. So at this point, we've scoped our MVP. We've clearly defined the problem we're trying to solve, our target user, our target use case, and our target data sources. The next step is to create a so-called golden data set. This is just a data set with pairs of queries and their relevant context. So, as an example for that YouTube answer engine that I'm building, that system takes in technical AI questions from users like what is masked language modeling, picking evals for LLM loops, why is overfitting a problem, and on and on and on. And the relevant context will be a video ID for one of my YouTube videos. And so, the golden data set serves as a ground truth that we can use to evaluate our retrieval system. So in other words, once we build our retrieval pipeline, we can pass in one of these questions and then compare the systems search results to this ground truth here. What this allows us to do is to systematically improve our rag system in a datadriven way. And this isn't just for like question answering system. This generalizes to any rag system that takes in any kind of user input. For example, if the input isn't a question, but some kind of insurance claim, and the system is supposed to match the input claim with a set of existing claims and to generate some kind of report or evaluation or whatever it is, that would be the user input here. And then the correct result and the relevant context would be all the things you would want retrieved by the retrieval system. So this idea generalizes you just want to create these pairs of user inputs. So in this case it was a question and the correct retrieval results. So in this case it was a video ID. And then a quick technical note here. It's important that you create a development testing split for your golden data set. All that means is you're going to reserve some percentage of your golden data set for the development work. So as you explore different design choices for the retrieval system or the answer system, you'll strictly look at results on your development set. And then when you want to compare different design choices together, you can do those evaluations on the testing split. This is important because if you use all of the golden data set to optimize your rag system, then there's a risk that you kind of overoptimize the system just on your golden data set and then it no longer generalizes to other types of user inputs. So that's what a golden data set is and why it's important. Next, let's talk about how we can actually create one of these. So the first way is to use real world queries. This is by far the best because this grounds your golden data set in reality as opposed to your impression of reality. The way this would work is you have users that are typing queries into some questionans answering system that's already existing and then you simply extract these queries and then manually curate the correct context and the correct search results for each of these queries. While this is the best because you are grounding the golden data set in real world queries, practically speaking, most people don't have an existing baseline for the rack system that they're building. So in these cases, we can turn to synthetic query generation. So the process here is you start with your inventory. So let's say you have a set of PDFs or documents or chunks or whatever it is. You'll then give these sources to an LLM one at a time to generate synthetic queries. But of course, there are a few gotchas here. So, it's important that you iterate on this query gen and don't just have an LLM write the prompt for you and you don't really look at it and then you're just off to the races generating queries. These queries are going to be the foundation of your rag system. So, if these synthetic queries are lowquality, your rag system is ultimately going to be lowquality. So spend time on the prompt that you're giving the LLM to generate these queries. There's a nice blog post by HL Hussein who talks about how to generate these queries for LLM as a judge system, but the same principles apply. But basically what he talks about is to define these different axes and different dimensions for the queries. For example, you could define the different personas as dimensions or the different use cases as dimensions or different features you want the system to have. You basically break each of these dimensions down and have the LLM generate a query for each of them. So let's say you have personas of like individual contributors and managers and then you have use cases of employee onboarding and then policy lookup. So basically the employees trying to look up if they're allowed to do something or not allowed to do something. Basically you'd have the LLM generate a query for each of these four possible situations. So a manager onboarding or manager policy lookup or individual contributor onboarding or individual contributor policy lookup and so on and so forth. And then a final tip is to ground in real world data if available. So even if you don't have data of users typing the inputs into a baseline system itself, you might have data of users typing in similar inputs into other systems that you could try to pull upon. So an example of this is the way I did the synthetic query generation for this YouTube answer engine. So the grounding here were the individual YouTube transcripts which correspond to the inventory of the rag system. But also for each of the videos I grabbed all of the YouTube comments and provided that as additional context to the LLM. And then I had three different types of query. I had exact or factual like what is decision trees, what is overfitting, what is error analysis, things like that. Conceptual which is more like why is this important or how does this idea connect to this other idea or whatever it is. And then procedural like how do I do X? How do I do error analysis? How do I fine-tune BERT? And so on and so forth. These are these are different types of questions, different types of sub use cases below the technical Q&A. And then I also had a difficulty. So I had grounded answers. So basically the answer was kind of a trivial thing based on a snippet of the YouTube transcript. I had very hard questions that included typos and the user was confused about something. because a lot of times when people don't understand a concept, their questions include false assumptions and things that are just not correct. And so I had the LM generate queries like that and then something in the middle and so I called those medium. What this resulted in is I had the video transcript and the comments as grounding and I had a prompt that instructed the model on how to write queries of each of these types at each of these difficulties. That results in nine queries per video and that's because each type will have all three difficulties. And then I went through and manually reviewed all the synthetic queries and narrowed it down to six queries per video. And the reason for this is that the synthetic queries aren't going to be perfect. So you want to have some kind of manual review process because in the case of this golden data set, quantity and quality are both important, but it's better to have a smaller data set of highquality examples as opposed to a bigger data set of lowquality examples. If you want to see the full prompt of the query gen and all the code for it, it's freely available on this GitHub repo. The link is shown here and I also have it linked in the description. It's reference number two. Now that we have our golden data set, the next step is to build the initial version of our retrieval system. This is just to serve as a baseline. So you'll take your source documents, you'll take your inventory, you'll create some kind of database, whether you want to do a vector database with any of the tools available today or you want to do lexical search, so you want to do keyword-based search and there are many options for that as well. This is not a complete list of tools. This is just off the top of my head and doing a little bit of research, but I'm sure there are many other tools out there. Or you can do a combination of vector search and lexical search. So actually for the YouTube answer engine, I did a hybrid search as a baseline. Because of all the tools available today, building this baseline retrieval system is super straightforward. These tools have abstractions for everything. What's more important, however, in step three is defining how you're going to evaluate the system. So not just building the retrieval system, but defining success of the retrieval system. This brings up the concept of retrieval evaluations. And so here I'm going to talk about three of the most popular ones. First is precision which is defined by this equation here. TP stands for true positive. FP stands for false positive. And all that means is if you have a set of retrieval results like let's say you retrieve five results. True positive just means you returned a result that was actually relevant to the input. False positive means you returned a result that is not relevant to the input. The next common eval is called recall at K and that's defined by this equation. So basically the numerator is the number of relevant results in the top K. K can be one, it can be three, it can be five, it can be 20, it can be whatever integer you like. So if K is let's say three, the numerator will be the number of relevant results in the top three results. And then the denominator is the total number of relevant results. So let's say you have one relevant result and it appears in the top three then the numerator will be one and then since you only have one relevant result in your whole database the denominator will be one. So recall at K is one. Recall is good when you don't care about the percentage of relevant documents in your results. You just care that the relevant documents are retrieved period. However, precision is more helpful when you care about that percentage. And then finally we have mean reciprocal rank or MRR for short. The equation for this is a bit more complicated but basically we have the total number of queries which is denoted by Q with these vertical bars on each side. And then we have the rank of the top most relevant chunk. And so mean reciprocal rank is different than precision recall because precision and recall can be evaluated at the query level. So like one query at a time while mean reciprocal rank is like an aggregate metric. So this is meant to evaluate multiple queries at once. I know these definitions are quite technical. So I wanted to give like English versions of them. So precision is just the percentage of retrieved chunks which are relevant. Recall is the percentage of all relevant chunks that are retrieved. And then finally MR captures the performance of a set of queries based on ranking. And so you don't have to use all of these. And most likely for your use case, there are some that are more appropriate than others. For the YouTube answer engine, I have a typo here, but for the YouTube answer engine, I used recall as the main eval. So if the question is, what is language modeling in bird? And the system retrieves three results. The true ID is this. Recall at one will be one because that means the true ID is the top result. Also, recall at three will be one because out of the top three results, the true ID is in there as well. However, for this next one, steps to do error analysis for AI app, the true ID was retrieved, but it was the third result, not the first result. So, recall at one is actually going to be zero because it's not the number one result, but recall at three is going to be one because it appears in the top three. And a similar thing for this query of rag versus these agents when to combine question mark recall at one is zero but recall at three is one. But then when the true ID does not appear in the top three results both recall at one and recall at three will be zero. And then to aggregate all these to get a sense of the retrieval systems performance on the entire data set here you can just take an average. So that's what I did here. So the average here is going to be 0.2 two and the average of recall at three is going to be 0.6. Now that we've built the baseline retrieval system, we can now build a baseline response system and have a endtoend rag system. Just a quick note here, it's important that steps three and four are separate and that's because the retrieval piece of your rag system and the response generation piece of your rag system are two independent components. It's helpful to build them separately and to evaluate them separately. For the response generation, the way this will work is we have our request. We have the endto-end rag system. So we have our LLM, which can be whatever LLM you like. These are just a few possible places you can get LLMs from, but there are many more. And then we'll have a prompt that combines the retrieved results and gives the LLM instructions for fulfilling the request. And then we have the LLM generate a response. And so there are actually two main ways we can build out this rag system. The first is what I'll call classic rag, which is where you have a user query come in. We use that query to kick off retrieval and then we give those retrieval results to the LLM. It's just this linear workflow of giving context to the LLM. This is very simple, but there are problems with this. Like if someone just asks the question, hi, how's it going? The system might kick off a retrieval when that's not necessary. or the user query is not really clear, but then it still kicks off a retrieval step and returns no results. And so these are of course things you can fix with query routing or query rewriting, but those are other steps you have to build into the system. More recently, now that LLM tool calling is commonplace, what's actually more popular is to do agentic rag. So instead of building out this linear workflow, you can just give a retrieval tool to the LLM and then the LLM can decide whenever it thinks it should grab context to do these retrievalss. And so this is a little simpler and you don't have to worry as much about query routing or rewriting user inputs and things like that. But this is also complicated because now there's a new possible failure mode where the agent kicks off a retrieval when it shouldn't have kicked off a retrieval or the inverse where the agent doesn't do retrieval when it should have done retrieval. Personally, I like to start with classic rag cuz at least you have the simplest version of your system. And then once you have a pretty decent classic rag system, making that transition to agentic rag is a little easier cuz you've already resolved a lot of the other failures that might come up. But just like how we did the evaluation of the retrieval system, now we can do endtoend evaluation of our rag system. So basically not just evaluating the search results, but also evaluating the responses. But this is kind of hard. So if we have a user input like definition of fine-tuning of machine learning and then we have a very long response from the model, we can't use precision here. We can't use recall here. There's no offtheshelf metric we can use to evaluate this text. Sure, there are some generic NLP metrics we could try to pull from, but these won't capture the nuances of what's going on here. So the question is, how do we evaluate open-ended responses? And the answer to this is error analysis, which is something I talked about in a previous video. But basically, error analysis in the world of large language models looks something like this, where you see the user's input, you see the model's output, and then you just manually review the responses, and you leave open-ended notes. Here's an example. For the most part, this response was good. But then there was this one subtle issue where the model said here, "An analogy frequently used is that fine-tuning is like refining a raw diamond where the pre-trained model is the raw stone and fine-tuning shapes it into a more valuable specialized purpose." And so it's kind of awkward that it's an analogy frequently used. That analogy might frequently be used by me in these different sources of my YouTube videos, but that's not actually something I've heard anywhere else. So it might be confusing that the model is responding in this way. And there are many other little weird quirks that come up as you read more and more of these responses. So a good starting point is to read at least 30 responses. But the more responses you read are better. Haml Hussein and Shreer Shankar they have a book on AI valves and they also are teaching a course on AI evals which I had the opportunity to take. They recommend reading through a hundred of these responses or traces when doing error analysis. And the reason you want to read so many is that the more responses you see, patterns begin to emerge. Maybe an analogy frequently used is just a quirk of this one response and it's not going to come up in any other responses. But there definitely are going to be patterns that come up over and over again. For example, in another response here, the query was training on my own instruct GPT process error model runs forever. The response is based on the provided transcripts, there's no mention of an instruct GPT process error or models running forever during training of instruct GPT. This was a failure that actually came up a lot. Basically, the model is responding as if the user provided the transcripts instead of the transcripts being provided by a internal retrieval tool. And so here my notes were user doesn't provide transcripts confusing response. This led me to create a specific failure tag for these situations which I called bad framing. Basically, the model is framing the response poorly because it's talking as if the user provided the transcripts. And then there were some other common failures that came up after looking at 50 responses. In some cases, just using way too complex of language, not explaining things in plain English, being too technical. basically was starting to write Python code that was outdated and just not helpful and then bad narrative. It didn't respond in a a well ststructured way. It was just kind of like a word vomit. And so these patterns will emerge as you write these notes. You see them come up over and over again. If something occurs more than once, you can give it a specialized tag. And so for doing this process, I created a custom data viewer that basically is everything I want. It makes it easy to navigate all this. I have the golden video, which is the one that generated this query. I have all the retrieved videos here. So each query gets retrieves three videos and then there's a separate section for which ones were actually cited. So the model didn't site any of them even though it could have probably come up with an answer probably from this video. And so this custom data viewer, it's a simple stream application and I just vibecoded it with claude. And since this is just an internal tool, it doesn't have to be perfect. It just has to be good enough to remove the friction of this data labeling process. But once you create these tags, you've identified a handful of common failure modes for your system. And so the next step is instead of doing this manually every single time is to figure out how to reliably automate these evaluations. There are two ways to do this. One is to create code-based evals. So for example, this bad framing failure. This was actually pretty easy to check for because there were some predictable patterns that would come up over and over again when the system would do this. So I could just do a simple string check to see if any of these bad patterns appeared in the model's response. But of course, not everything can be resolved with simple string checks. For example, if I wanted to create a evaluation for a bad narrative, basically the model is just doing some kind of word vomit, not following a structured narrative, I might need to use a LLM based check for this. And so the way that would work is you will take the response, you'll give it to an LLM judge, and the LLM judge will evaluate the pass or fail of that evaluation. While the LLM judge might sound like magic, there's of course a gotcha here, which is that the alignment of the LLM judge is not trivial. Most times when you create the LLM judge, its labels will not be aligned with the manual labels that you created. So in this case, they agree on these two, but they don't agree on these two. So if you do have to create an LLM judge, it's important that you spend a lot of time on the alignment of the LLM's labels to the manual ones that you generated through the error analysis process. And so this is kind of like a mini project within your AI project. But it's important that you get it right because again, this evaluation will guide the development of your rag system. So if you have a lowquality LLM judge, you're going to have a lowquality rag system with respect to this failure mode. But just some pros and cons here. So codebased evals are super simple and easy to implement. On the other hand, LLM based evals are more error tolerant because we can imagine there's going to be some bad framing that doesn't include one of these really easy string checks. Maybe it's some other weird one that hasn't been seen before. An LM judge might be able to handle that edge case more gracefully. But despite that, I think it's better to just use the codebased eval whenever possible because it's much easier to implement. There's kind of like a beauty in the simplicity because you know it's simple. You kind of understand where it can break and where it might not break. While the LLM judge, it may not be obvious where the LLM judge is reliable or not reliable. And then finally, if you do have to use the LLM judge, again, create a dev test split so you're not overfitting on a single set of labels. What I mean by this is let's say you labeled 50 responses manually. Don't use all 50 to evaluate your LLM judge. You could do like a 60/40 split where you use 30 of the labels to drive the development of your LLM judge, but then you can use 20 of the examples to actually evaluate its performance. To give a concrete example, this YouTube answer engine evaluite, I had the mean recall at one and mean recall at three. So these are the retrieval metrics, but then I created this codebased eval for the bad framing which was actually happening 50% of the time. So these are real numbers from this system. So at this point we've built the initial version of our endto-end rag system. And we have an initial eval suite that we can use to systematically evaluate this application. And this sets us up nicely for step five, which is to run experiments. Now we can create different versions of our rag system whether we're changing some part of the retrieval system or we're changing our prompt or we're changing a model or whatever it is. Now we have our baseline which is our initial version of the system. And then in experiment two let's say I change the prompt to try to correct for this bad framing failure and then this failure drops significantly. So it goes from happening 50% of the time to happening 7% of the time which is a big improvement. And then let's say I do some good data processing so I'm not just using the raw transcripts. I'm removing stop words. I'm making everything lowercase and doing some data hygiene stuff. And then maybe that'll improve the search results so that recall at one and recall at three significantly increase as well. And then on and on and on. I actually did experiments one and two. I haven't done experiment three yet. But at this phase, the most important thing is to design your project for experimentation as opposed to production because these are very different modalities and goals. And so a lot of times when you're building a rag system, you might be building it with production in mind. You're trying to design the system as if you're creating an application that you're going to host somewhere and users are going to interact with. But when you're building a rag system, there's really no way to know what the optimal design choices are going to be at the outset. You have to run these experiments to figure out what the best design choices are going to be. And that's why you want to remove all the friction for running experiments quickly. But that said, you don't want to go too crazy where you start changing like five different things from run one to two or two to three and so on and so forth. It's important that you change one thing at a time because if you're changing more than one thing, it's not going to be obvious what change is impacting each of these evaluation metrics. But once we accept this experimental approach to building rack systems, the next obvious question is what changes do I make? What experiments am I running? There are several different components to a rag system that will influence its performance. But almost always the best place to start is the retrieval system. So basically how you're building out your database, whether it's a vector database or just a traditional database. Almost always the problem is in the source documents. So usually better extraction, better pre-processing, better chunking, better sources is going to lead to better performance of the overall system. But also there are a lot of other things you can do. So you can have specialized indexes. So you can have indexes for different data modalities like for images or transcripts or meeting notes or invoices. You can have an index for different use cases. So if you have one for onboarding questions, you can have an index for policy questions. You can have an index for technology questions. You can play around with the different retrieval parameters, play around with embedding models, tagging chunks, and doing some kind of hybrid search with the meta tags. You can add rerankers. You can also improve the query part. So you can do query rewriting, or you can just jump to a rag and have the LLM write the queries when it uses this retrieval tool. Then finally, you can improve the prompt you're sending to your LLM. You can use a more powerful LLM or you can do query routing where you have different LLMs that tend to do better for different types of questions. But obviously just looking at this, the number of design choices we could make when building a rag system is just tremendous. This is why it's important to have evaluations and to do error analysis because the improvements that you make to your system should be driven by specific failures. So, for example, when I jumped from experiment one to experiment two, I was specifically trying to improve this bad framing rate by updating the prompt to just give the model more context that this is an internal tool that's fetching the results. It's not being provided by the user. And then just a few tweaks to the prompt led to a significant drop in this bad framing rate. Or if the goal is to improve recall at three, then I'm going to be focusing on aspects of the retrieval system. So the next thing I might explore is doing better pre-processing because there are a lot of problems with the transcripts that I get from YouTube. So maybe I'm going to play around with like lemitization or I'm going to explore some techniques there and that'll be experiment three. But maybe that doesn't give me the best results. So I go to chunking and try to create smaller chunks like two to five minute chunks of each video and then I do retrieval over that and see if that results in any kind of lift and then so on and so forth. When trying to make a improvement to your system, start with the failure that you're trying to correct and have a metric that is a representation of that failure. And so now you have a objective quantity that you're trying to improve with whatever change you're making. And if you're making one change at a time, it should be obvious what is helping and what is not helping.
